title: Was Gendo ultimately good or ultimately bad
display_title: Was Gendo ultimately good or ultimately bad
wikitext:
[]: Was Gendo ultimately good or ultimately bad?             Quote Post by CyberXIII » Wed Jul 20, 2011 2:56 pm The title says it all. Without a doubt, Gendo's one of the most conflicted, misunderstood, complicated characters in the whole franchise. Some say he's a horrible monster who should have been put down a long time ago (I used to be in this category), others say he's a sympathetic tragic hero like his son. What do you all think: was he really a bad guy in the end or was he a good guy stuck in tragic circumstances? Keep in mind everything he's done was to be reunited with his dead wife above all else.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:09 pm He is Neutral Evil. At least, in "Dungeons and Dragons" terms. Wikipedia wrote: Neutral Evil is called the "Malefactor" alignment. Characters of this alignment are typically selfish and have no qualms about turning on their allies-of-the-moment. They have no compunctions about harming others to get what they want, but neither will they go out of their way to cause carnage or mayhem when they see no direct benefit to it. They abide by laws for only as long as it is convenient for them. A villain of this alignment can be more dangerous than either Lawful or Chaotic Evil characters, since he is neither bound by any sort of honor or tradition nor disorganized and pointlessly violent. Examples are an assassin who has little regard for formal laws but does not needlessly kill, a henchman who plots behind his superior's back, or a mercenary who switches sides if made a better offer. Complete Scoundrel cites X-Men's Mystique, and Sawyer of the early seasons of Lost as Neutral Evil characters.[7] Yugoloths (Daemons) are the outsiders of Neutral Evil.

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:32 pm Neither. His end goal was noble, but he did many evil things to get there.

[]: Re: Was Gendo ultimately good or ultimately bad?             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:36 pm CyberXIII wrote: The title says it all. Without a doubt, Gendo's one of the most conflicted, misunderstood, complicated characters in the whole franchise. Some say he's a horrible monster who should have been put down a long time ago (I used to be in this category), others say he's a sympathetic tragic hero like his son. What do you all think: was he really a bad guy in the end or was he a good guy stuck in tragic circumstances? Keep in mind everything he's done was to be reunited with his dead wife above all else.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:44 pm TehDonutKing wrote: His end goal was noble

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:49 pm esselfortium wrote: [citation needed]

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:51 pm Assuming no concurrency, and EoTV being Gendou's good end, he seems to have a noble goal.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Jul 20, 2011 3:59 pm TehDonutKing wrote: Assuming no concurrency, and EoTV being Gendou's good end, he seems to have a noble goal.

[]:             Quote Post by Tarnsman » Wed Jul 20, 2011 4:14 pm Stryker wrote: Lawl. Re-uniting with your wife isn't noble no more.

[]:             Quote Post by CyberXIII » Wed Jul 20, 2011 4:29 pm Tarnsman wrote: How was is it ever noble? He's doing it based on his own selfish desires. Classifying people based on D&D alignments doesn't really work because 99% would fall into neutral evil.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Wed Jul 20, 2011 4:33 pm He's good. Though in a misunderstood way.

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Wed Jul 20, 2011 5:00 pm He isn't good or evil. He's just human.

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Wed Jul 20, 2011 5:27 pm child of Lilith wrote: He isn't good or evil. He's just human.

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Wed Jul 20, 2011 5:55 pm He's a dick.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Wed Jul 20, 2011 6:05 pm child of Lilith wrote: He isn't good or evil. He's just human.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Jul 20, 2011 6:18 pm He's cold and ruthless but not especially evil, really. Sure his main goal is a selfish one but it never actually gets in the way of his role in saving humanity from both the angels and from Seele. It's not exactly an altruistic act since he's protecting himself as well, but it certainly ought to score him some consideration.

[]:             Quote Post by Stryker » Wed Jul 20, 2011 6:49 pm Tarnsman wrote: How was is it ever noble? He's doing it based on his own selfish desires.

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Wed Jul 20, 2011 7:05 pm I'm a bit surprised that this saying hasn't been mentioned yet, as it's the perfect summation of Gendo's motivations and actions: The road to hell is paved with good intentions. To say that he is good or evil is a dicey subject, but there's little doubt that his final objective was "good". The methods he used to obtain said goal are from noble however; to him, the ends justify the means. Whether or not that perspective is good or evil is very subjective, but it certainly is human.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jul 20, 2011 10:48 pm I don't know if being a jerk counts for being evil/villainous. Japanese animation seems to diminish in clear-cut heroes and villains after it reaches a certain age within its target audience. The older the target audience, the more it seems to just depict the opposing goals of characters than it does good vs evil, and I really like how Evangelion does this. If Gendo is "Good", and SEELE was opposed to Gendo in the latter half of the series, then SEELE was "Bad". But they both wanted Third Impact, which nobody else on Earth really wanted, so both Gendo and SEELE could be seen as "Bad". And since Shinji is doing whatever his dad tells him to do for most of the series, this means that Shinji was also "Bad". In fact, by that definition everyone in Evangelion is "Bad" for the majority of the series. (Except for poor, innocent Pen-Pen and Hikari. Even Kensuke wanted in on NERV and stuff.) But Gendo only wanted to be reunited with his wife, which is "Good". Shinji wants to be reunited with his mother at some point in the series, which is also "Good". SEELE didn't want any of that and tried to keep them from those goals throughout most of the series, so they could be "Bad". But even Shinji and Gendo's "Good" intentions conflict with each other during the last half of the series. So which character is "Good" and which character is "Bad" in wanting any of this, and who should "Win" based on his "Good" merits? But SEELE wasn't all that "Bad" if you consider that all they wanted was for the human race to evolve to the next step. Evolution is a "Good" and natural part of life, even if it must be ignited intentionally by man, right? Shinji didn't want any of that by the end of EoE, so I guess he could be considered "Bad", right? Even if it was "Good" for him because his mother said it was "Good"? And now I'm thoroughly confused... Everyone's selfish, everyone's needy, and therefore everyone's trying to have their own goals trump the goals of the others by any means possible. Even if it means Yui has to reject the Dummy System and absorb Shinji, or if it means Gendo has to put his son through terrible, terrible things, even if it means forcing Third Impact on a world that has no idea what's really going on and asked for none of it, offing Kaji in order to get him out of the way for it all to happen, and even if it means changing Third Impact for everyone just so Shinji can sit on a beach somewhere and be in a world with emotional barriers and definable walls. And I find that to be the best way to look at the characters in the Evangelion series.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Jul 20, 2011 11:14 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Japanese animation seems to diminish in clear-cut heroes and villains after it reaches a certain age within its target audience. The older the target audience, the more it seems to just depict the opposing goals of characters than it does good vs evil,
